this path-breaking book, Berger offers bold reconsideration twentieth century black activism, prison system, origins mass incarceration.
Throughout civil rights era, black activists thrust prison into public view, turning prisoners into symbols racial oppression while arguing that confinement inescapable part black life United States.
Black prisoners became global political icons time when notions race nation were flux.
Showing that prison central focus black radical imagination from 1950s through 1980s, Berger traces dynamic dramatic history this political struggle.
prison shaped rise spread black activism, from civil rights demonstrators willfully risking arrests many current former prisoners that built joined organizations such Black Panther Party.
Grounded extensive research, Berger engagingly demonstrates that such organizing made prison walls porous influenced generations activists that followed.
